deck_id: Lecture 1 Foundations Bayes MLE and ERM.pdf

results.stem: A student submits CA1 121 hours after the deadline and claims they should only lose 50 points (10 points per 24 hours) and still receive some credit. Based on the stated grading policy, what outcome is consistent with the rules, and why?

student_A.memory_reflection: I mostly remember there was something about grading and deadlines, and also a code of conduct/integrity slide. I don’t remember the exact late policy details.

option_analysis.A: Has a super specific cutoff (5 days / 120 hours) which sounds like something a strict deadline policy would have. Also it kind of mentions both rules together.
option_analysis.B: Says it’s strictly linear, but I don’t actually remember that being true. Feels too nice/flexible for deadlines.
option_analysis.C: Mentions integrity policy, which I do remember was a slide, but it doesn’t sound like it’s about late submissions.
option_analysis.D: Rounding up fractions sounds plausible but I don’t remember anything about rounding rules.

student_A.reasoning: Since I barely remember the details, I’m guessing the course had a hard cutoff for lateness because that’s a common strict deadline thing. Option A mentions a clear '5 days/120 hours' rule, and 121 hours is past that, so I’ll go with A.

student_B.memory_reflection: I remember they said deadlines are strict and late work loses marks. I don’t recall the exact numbers, but I do remember there was a strong rule that very late work can get zero. I also remember plagiarism is a zero, but that’s separate from lateness.

option_analysis.A: This mentions a specific cutoff at 5 days (120 hours) leading to ZERO. That sounds like the kind of strict rule they emphasized (hard cutoff). Even though I don’t explicitly remember ‘120 hours’, it fits ‘deadlines are strict’ plus ‘late work loses marks’ and sounds like a real policy detail.
option_analysis.B: This assumes it’s only linear with no cutoff. That feels less consistent with the ‘strict’ vibe from the lecture, and policies usually have some maximum penalty.
option_analysis.C: Academic integrity was about plagiarism/collaboration, not lateness. So this seems off; lateness definitely had penalties.
option_analysis.D: Rounding up to the next 24-hour block sounds plausible, but I don’t remember anything about rounding rules. Also it doesn’t address the possibility of a hard cutoff, which I think is more likely.

student_B.reasoning: Since the lecture stressed strict deadlines and serious penalties, the option that matches that strictness is the one with a hard cutoff to zero after 5 days. The student is 121 hours late, which is just over 120 hours, so under that kind of rule they’d get zero even if there is also a per-day deduction rule for smaller delays. The exact ‘10 points per 24 hours’ part seems like it would apply only before hitting the cutoff.

student_C.memory_reflection: From the memory trace: late penalty is minus 10 points out of 100 per 24 hours late, and there is also a hard rule that if a submission is 5 days (120 hours) late or more, it gets a zero mark. These are stated as strict late penalties, separate from plagiarism/academic integrity issues.

option_analysis.A: Matches the explicit cutoff: 121 hours is >= 120 hours (5 days), so the zero-mark rule applies even if the linear penalty would suggest otherwise.
option_analysis.B: Conflicts with the memory trace because it ignores the stated hard cutoff at 120 hours.
option_analysis.C: Incorrect because lateness penalties are clearly specified; academic integrity rules are a separate issue and don't replace late penalties.
option_analysis.D: The memory trace does not mention rounding up partial days; also at 121 hours the cutoff to zero would apply anyway.

student_C.reasoning: The policy includes both a per-24-hour deduction and a hard cutoff: “zero mark if submitted 5 days (120 hours) late or more.” Since 121 hours exceeds 120 hours, the submission should receive zero, regardless of the student’s claim about losing only 50 points.

student_A: I remember something about grading and deadlines, but I didn’t really pay attention.
There was also a slide about code of conduct or integrity, I think.
student_B: The course grade had continuous assessment pieces plus a final exam, and the final was the biggest portion.
There was a CA1 early in the semester, a quiz during a lecture, and a CA2 that wasn’t fixed yet.
They said deadlines are strict and late work loses marks, and plagiarism gets you a zero.
AI tools like ChatGPT were mentioned as allowed for some help, but not to produce the whole submission.
The code of conduct emphasized participating and not sharing solutions or collaborating in unauthorized ways.
student_C: Continuous Assessment has three components: CA1 (15%), a Quiz (5%), and CA2 (20%), and the Final is 60%.
CA1 runs from 29 Aug 2025 to 3 Oct 2025 (Weeks 3–7) with a deadline at 23:59 SGT, and it will be announced in Week 3.
The Quiz is on 18 Sep 2025 during the Week 6 lecture; it is MCQ, fully open book, maximum 60 minutes, and conducted via Canvas.
CA2 details are TBA by Dr. Wang Si and is worth 20%.
The Final is closed book but allows one-page double-sided cheat sheet.
Late penalties are strict: minus 10 points out of 100 per 24 hours late, and zero mark if submitted 5 days (120 hours) late or more.

Plagiarism leads to a zero and a formal report for investigation; GPT-based tools (ChatGPT/Co-pilot/Gemini) are allowed for refinement/coding with amendments, but not for generating the entire project, and students must submit only their own work without sharing solutions or unauthorized collaboration, with violations reported to the university disciplinary panel.

results.stem: You want practice that most closely matches the exam condition where you may consult limited prepared material but not the textbook/notes freely. Which activity best aligns with that constraint?

student_A.memory_reflection: I mostly remember there was a slide about assessment/grading and something about tutorials being available, but I don’t remember any rules about open/closed book or cheat sheets.

option_analysis.A: Mentions a cheat sheet thing. Sounds kind of specific and exam-like, but I don’t really remember it.
option_analysis.B: CA1 sounds like an assessment and ‘strict deadlines’ feels like exam conditions to me, even though I’m not sure.
option_analysis.C: Tutorials being not graded is the only thing I vaguely remember, so this option feels familiar.
option_analysis.D: Says fully open book. That sounds like the opposite of an exam, but I’m not sure what the final allows.

student_A.reasoning: Since I don’t actually know the exam rules, I’m going with the option that matches the only thing I vaguely recall (tutorials being not graded), and I’m kind of assuming that makes it similar to exam practice somehow.

student_B.memory_reflection: I remember the quiz was open book and done during lecture on Canvas. I also remember tutorials weren’t graded and were meant to help and relate to final exam topics and question styles. I don’t clearly remember the exact final exam rules except that the exam is the main component of the grade. Nothing in my trace explicitly says ‘one-page double-sided cheat sheet’, but the question mentions limited prepared material which sounds like a cheat sheet idea.

option_analysis.A: This directly matches the idea of limited prepared material (a one-page double-sided cheat sheet). Even though I don’t explicitly remember this detail from the trace, it sounds like the exact exam condition described.
option_analysis.B: I remember strict late penalties, but that doesn’t imply closed book. Deadlines and book policy feel unrelated, so this seems like a trap.
option_analysis.C: I remember tutorials are not graded and help with exam style, but ‘not graded’ doesn’t mean they use the same closed-book rules. This feels like mixing up grading with exam conditions.
option_analysis.D: I remember quiz was open book, but the question wants limited prepared material (not fully open book). So ‘fully open book’ seems less similar to the constraint.

student_B.reasoning: The question is about practicing under a constraint where you can consult limited prepared material but not freely use textbook/notes. From the options, the only one that explicitly says something like that is the final exam with a cheat sheet. I do remember the quiz being open book, but that sounds too permissive compared to ‘limited prepared material’. Tutorials being not graded doesn’t really connect to book rules. So A seems to match the condition best.

student_C.memory_reflection: From the memory trace: the Final exam is described as closed book but allows a one-page double-sided cheat sheet. The Quiz is explicitly fully open book. CA1 is an assessment running Weeks 3–7 with strict late penalties, but nothing says it is closed/open book. Tutorials are not graded and are relevant practice, but no exam-like material rules are stated for them.

option_analysis.A: Matches the described condition: limited prepared material (one-page double-sided cheat sheet) but not freely consulting textbook/notes (closed book).
option_analysis.B: Strict deadlines relate to submission timing, not what materials you can consult. The memory trace does not say CA1 is closed book like the final.
option_analysis.C: Tutorials being not graded does not imply they use closed-book rules. The memory trace only says they start Week 3, discussed Week 4, and are relevant to finals, not that they mimic exam material constraints.
option_analysis.D: Quiz is fully open book, which conflicts with the final being closed book (except cheat sheet). So it does not match the limited-material condition.

student_C.reasoning: The question asks for practice matching an exam condition where you can consult limited prepared material but not freely use textbook/notes. The only activity in the memory trace with that exact constraint is the Final: closed book with a one-page double-sided cheat sheet. Quiz is the opposite (fully open book), and CA1/tutorials have no stated matching rules about allowed materials.

student_A: There was a slide about assessment and grading, I think.
Something about tutorials being available, but I don’t remember details.
student_B: The course grade was mostly the final exam, with some continuous assessment parts like CA1, a quiz, and CA2.
I remember there were strict late penalties and plagiarism would get you a zero.
The quiz was during a lecture and it was open book, probably multiple choice, and done online (Canvas).

student_C: Continuous Assessment had three components: CA1 (15%), a Quiz (5%), and CA2 (20%), and the Final was 60%.
CA1 ran from 29 Aug 2025 to 3 Oct 2025 at 23:59 SGT (Weeks 3–7) and was to be announced in Week 3.
The Quiz was on 18 Sep 2025 during the Week 6 lecture, MCQ, fully open book, maximum 60 minutes, and conducted via Canvas.

results.stem: A student wants to speak specifically with Dr. XU Yuecong during the portion of the course he teaches. Which consultation slot is explicitly scheduled during his teaching weeks?

student_A.memory_reflection: I honestly only remember a welcome slide and something vague about lectures and office hours, but I don't remember any exact weeks or times.

option_analysis.A: Has a very specific time and weeks, but I don't remember Week 2/5/6 at all.
option_analysis.B: Also very specific, but Week 8/10/12 doesn't ring a bell either.
option_analysis.C: This one sounds like it matches the vague thing about '2nd part, Wk 7–13' and feels more like the kind of summary I might have seen.
option_analysis.D: Super specific with a date (25 Aug). I definitely don't remember a date like that.

student_A.reasoning: I don't really know, so I'm picking the option that matches the only thing I vaguely recall ('2nd part, Wk 7–13'). Option C literally says that, so it feels safest even though I'm basically guessing.

student_B.memory_reflection: From the trace I remember: the course is split into two parts taught by two lecturers (early vs later). There are consultation hours on Mondays in an ECE conference room. I don’t remember exact week numbers or exact time slots, just that it’s Monday consultation and there’s a later lecturer for the second part.

option_analysis.A: This gives specific Monday time and some early-ish weeks (2,5,6). My memory only says one lecturer is early semester, so these weeks sound like the first part lecturer, not Dr. XU (who I think is the later one).
option_analysis.B: This is a Monday slot with later weeks (8,10,12). Since my memory says one lecturer teaches later in the semester, this feels like it matches Dr. XU’s teaching weeks more.
option_analysis.C: This sounds familiar because it states '2nd part, Wk 7–13', which matches the idea of later in the semester. But it says 'any week' which feels too vague compared to 'explicitly scheduled' consultation slots.
option_analysis.D: This is super specific (Week 7 date) but my memory trace doesn’t mention any exact single-week consultation like that. Also it’s 13:00–14:30, while I vaguely recall consultation was Monday but not this time.

student_B.reasoning: I know Dr. XU is the lecturer for the later portion (second part) of the course. The question asks for an explicitly scheduled consultation slot during his teaching weeks, so I should pick the option that lists a Monday slot in later weeks. Even though option C has the '2nd part, Wk 7–13' phrase that matches my memory, 'any week' doesn’t sound like an explicit scheduled slot. So I’m leaning to the later-week specific schedule in option B.

student_C.memory_reflection: From the memory trace: Dr. XU Yuecong teaches Part I (Week 1–6). Consultation is walk-in on Mondays 14:00–15:30 in Weeks 2, 5, and 6 at ECE Conference Room E4-05-39. There is also a Monday 13:00–14:30 slot in Week 3 (25 Aug) at an ECE Conference Room. Announcements may change times; Q&A via shared Google Doc.

option_analysis.A: Matches the explicitly stated walk-in consultation: Mondays 14:00–15:30 in Weeks 2, 5, and 6, which fall within Dr. XU's teaching weeks (Part I, Weeks 1–6).
option_analysis.B: Weeks 8, 10, 12 are in Part II (Weeks 7–13), which is taught by Dr. WANG Si per the memory trace, so this doesn't fit.
option_analysis.C: Contradicts the memory trace: Dr. XU teaches Part I (Weeks 1–6), not Weeks 7–13. So this is wrong.
option_analysis.D: Week 7 is not mentioned for the 13:00–14:30 slot; the memory trace says Week 3 (25 Aug). Also Week 7 would be Part II, not Dr. XU's portion.

student_C.reasoning: The question asks for a consultation slot explicitly scheduled during Dr. XU's teaching weeks. The memory trace clearly lists a walk-in slot on Mondays 14:00–15:30 for Weeks 2, 5, and 6, which are within Weeks 1–6 (Part I). The other options either use Part II weeks or misstate the week/date for the 13:00–14:30 slot. Therefore A is the correct choice.

student_A: I remember a welcome slide for EE5907, but not much else.
Something about lectures and maybe office hours.
student_B: The course is EE5907 and it’s taught in two parts by two different lecturers, one early in the semester and one later.
The topics are about machine learning-ish things like classifiers/regressors, PCA/LDA, clustering, boosting, and SVM.
Lecture notes are posted around class time and recordings are available after class for review.
There’s a long in-person lecture with a break in the middle, and they mentioned consultation hours on Mondays in an ECE conference room.
The syllabus is on Canvas and there’s some shared Google Doc link for Q&A.
student_C: EE5907 is split between Dr. XU Yuecong for Part I (Week 1–6, until Recess) and Dr. WANG Si for Part II (Week 7–13).
Dr. XU Yuecong’s email is yc.xu@nus.edu.sg, and the slide also mentioned Graduate Assistants (GA).
The course covers decision theory, linear/non-linear classifiers and regressors, PCA, LDA, clustering, boosting, SVM, and related topics.
Lectures are in-person; notes are released right before each class and recordings are available at the end of each class for revision.
Each lecture runs 2.5 hours with a 15-minute break in between, and after-class discussion is welcomed.
Consultation is walk-in on Mondays: 14:00–15:30 in Weeks 2, 5, and 6 at ECE Conference Room E4-05-39, plus 13:00–14:30 in Week 3 (25 Aug) at an ECE Conference Room.
They said announcements would be made if consultation times change, and Q&A is via a shared Google Doc link.

results.stem: A student plans their workload by prioritizing only items that directly contribute to the course grade. Which plan correctly distinguishes graded components from non-graded support activities while still acknowledging exam relevance?

student_A.memory_reflection: I mostly remember there was a slide about assessment/grading, and something about a quiz and tutorials. I don’t remember exact breakdowns, just that quizzes exist and tutorials are a thing.

option_analysis.A: Sounds too extreme saying only final counts. Also I vaguely remember continuous assessment being a thing, so this feels off.
option_analysis.B: Mentions tutorials and continuous assessment components. Tutorials helping for finals sounds like what tutorials do, so this feels kinda plausible.
option_analysis.C: Lists a bunch of graded items (CA1, Quiz, CA2, Final) and says tutorials not graded but relevant. It’s long and specific, which makes me suspicious because I don’t remember details like CA1/CA2.
option_analysis.D: Says quiz not graded because open book—sounds like a random rule. Tutorials graded because discussed in class also sounds weird.

student_A.reasoning: I don’t really know the real grading scheme. I’m going to pick B because it connects tutorials with continuous assessment and finals prep, which matches my vague memory that tutorials and quizzes were mentioned together as course stuff.

student_B.memory_reflection: I remember the grade was mostly the final exam, plus a couple of continuous assessments and a quiz. Tutorials were explicitly said to not be graded, but they help understanding and preparing for the final. The quiz was open book on Canvas during lecture, but I don’t recall that meaning it’s ungraded.

option_analysis.A: Doesn’t match: I remember there WERE continuous assessments and a quiz that counted, not just the final. Also ‘closed book’ wasn’t in my memory trace.
option_analysis.B: Seems wrong: tutorials weren’t graded per my memory, so they shouldn’t be counted as continuous assessment components.
option_analysis.C: Matches: it lists CA1, Quiz, CA2, and Final as graded (sounds like ‘a couple CAs plus a quiz’), and it correctly says tutorials are not graded but still relevant for finals.
option_analysis.D: Doesn’t match: open book quiz being ungraded isn’t something I remember, and tutorials being graded contradicts my memory trace.

student_B.reasoning: From what I recall, the graded parts are the final exam plus continuous assessments and a quiz. Tutorials are support activities: not graded but still important for preparing for the final. Option C is the only one that cleanly separates graded components from tutorials while still acknowledging tutorials help with the final.

student_C.memory_reflection: From the memory trace: the graded breakdown is CA1 (15%), Quiz (5%), CA2 (20%), and Final (60%). Tutorials were explicitly said to be ungraded, but relevant to finals (not necessarily fully overlapping). The quiz being open book doesn’t make it ungraded; it still counts 5%.

option_analysis.A: Incorrect: memory trace says continuous assessment has graded parts (CA1, Quiz, CA2) totaling 40%, not practice-only.
option_analysis.B: Incorrect: tutorials were described as ungraded support; they are not one of the three CA components (which are CA1, Quiz, CA2).
option_analysis.C: Correct: matches the listed graded components (CA1, Quiz, CA2, Final) and correctly treats tutorials as not graded but still relevant to finals.
option_analysis.D: Incorrect: quiz is graded (5%) even though open book; tutorials are ungraded even if discussed in class.

student_C.reasoning: The memory trace clearly lists the graded components as CA1, Quiz, CA2, and the Final, with specific percentages. It also states tutorials are ungraded but relevant to finals. Therefore the plan that separates graded items from tutorials while still acknowledging their exam relevance is option C.

results.stem: You want to verify that the course covers both (i) Bayesian decision theory and (ii) support vector machines, and you also want to know where the official syllabus is posted. Which combined statement is consistent with the slides?

student_A.memory_reflection: I basically only remember a slide saying “Welcome to EE5907.” I vaguely remember hearing something about SVM, but I don’t really know details. I don’t remember anything about where the syllabus is posted.

option_analysis.A: Mentions SVM (support vector machine) which is a word I kind of remember. Also sounds like a normal place to put a syllabus (Canvas).
option_analysis.B: Q&A Google Doc sounds random. Also says not SVM, which clashes with my vague SVM memory.
option_analysis.C: Syllabus only right before each class sounds weird and chaotic. It does mention SVM though.
option_analysis.D: Week 3 only and neither topic sounds kind of unlikely, but I have no clue.

student_A.reasoning: Since I don’t really know, I’m picking the option that contains the one term I vaguely remember (SVM) and also sounds like the most standard/normal setup for a course (syllabus on Canvas).

student_B.memory_reflection: I remember the course topics list included decision theory (which I think is basically Bayesian decision theory) and also SVM. I also remember there is a Google Doc link used for Q&A, and that lecture notes are posted before class and recordings after. I don't specifically remember exactly where an official syllabus is posted.

option_analysis.A: Matches my memory that the course includes decision theory/Bayesian decision theory and SVM. 'Canvas (Pages tab)' is not in my memory trace, but it sounds like a normal official place for a syllabus, so it seems plausible.
option_analysis.B: Google Doc is in my memory trace, but it was for Q&A, not necessarily the official syllabus. Also it says not SVM, which conflicts with my memory that SVM is covered.
option_analysis.C: Notes are posted before class, but I don't remember the syllabus being only distributed right before each class. Also it says not Bayesian decision theory/decision theory, which conflicts with my memory.
option_analysis.D: Doesn't match because I remember decision theory and SVM are covered, so saying neither are part of learning outcomes seems wrong. Also Week 3 announcement isn't in my memory.

student_B.reasoning: From the topics I recall, the course definitely covers decision theory (sounds like Bayesian decision theory) and SVM. For the syllabus location, I only clearly remember the Q&A Google Doc, but that option messes up the SVM part. So I go with the option that correctly includes both Bayesian decision theory and SVM, and the Canvas/Pages thing sounds like where a syllabus would be posted even though I don't explicitly remember it.

student_C.memory_reflection: From the slides I remember: core topics explicitly included decision theory (including Bayesian decision theory) and also SVM. The syllabus was said to be available on Canvas under the Pages tab. Notes are released right before each class, but that was about lecture notes/recordings, not the syllabus.

option_analysis.A: Matches the memory trace: syllabus on Canvas (Pages tab) and topics include Bayesian decision theory and SVM.
option_analysis.B: Doesn't match: I don't recall any Q&A Google Doc for the syllabus, and SVM was listed as a core topic/learning outcome.
option_analysis.C: Incorrect: syllabus wasn't only distributed right before each class (that was notes). Also Bayesian decision theory was included, so 'not Bayesian' is wrong.
option_analysis.D: Incorrect: syllabus location wasn't 'announced only in Week 3' and both Bayesian decision theory and SVM were part of the listed topics/learning outcomes.

student_C.reasoning: The slides explicitly said the syllabus is on Canvas under Pages, and the listed core topics/learning outcomes included both Bayesian decision theory and SVM. Only option A combines these correctly.

student_A: I remember a slide that said “Welcome to EE5907.”
There was something about lectures and maybe SVM, but I don’t really know.
student_B: EE5907 is taught by two professors, one for the first half and another for the later weeks.
The course covers pattern recognition topics like decision theory, classifiers/regressors, PCA, LDA, clustering, boosting, and SVM.
Lecture notes are posted before class and recordings come out after class, and there’s a break during the lecture.
There are some Monday consultation hours in an ECE conference room, and a Google Doc link is used for Q&A.
The learning outcomes are basically to understand these methods, apply them to real data for classification/regression, and compare their pros and cons.
student_C: The welcome slide said EE5907 is conducted by Dr. XU Yuecong for the first part (up to Recess) and Dr. Wang Si for the second part (Weeks 7–13).
It listed core topics: decision theory (incl. Bayesian decision theory), linear and non-linear classifiers and regressors, PCA, LDA, clustering, boosting, and SVM.
The syllabus is available on Canvas under the Pages tab.
For in-person lectures, notes are released right before each class and the recording becomes available at the end of each class for revision.
The lecture runs 2.5 hours with a 15-minute break in the middle, and after-class discussion is welcomed.

Consultation is walk-in on Mondays: 14:00–15:30 in Weeks 2, 5, and 6 at ECE Conference Room E4-05-39, plus 13:00–14:30 in Week 3 (25 Aug) at an ECE conference room, with announcements if times change.

Learning outcomes were: understand core pattern-recognition techniques (Bayesian decision theory, linear/nonlinear classifiers, PCA, LDA, clustering, SVM), apply conceptual/mathematical tools to real-world data for feature extraction and classification/regression, and assess strengths/weaknesses of different algorithms.
